text: After the death of Josue, the children of Israel consulted the Lord, saying: Who shall go up before us against the Chanaanite, and shall be the leader of the war?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said: Juda shall go up: behold I have delivered the land into his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda said to Simeon, his brother: Come up with me into my lot, and fight against the Chanaanite, that I also may go along with thee into thy lot. And Simeon went with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda went up, and the Lord delivered the Chanaanite, and the Pherezite into their hands: and they slew of them in Bezec ten thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they found Adonibezec in Bezec, and fought against him, and they defeated the Chanaanite, and the Pherezite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Adonibezec fled: and they pursued after him and took him, and cut off his fingers and toes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Adonibezec said: Seventy kings, having their fingers and toes cut off, gathered up the leavings of the meat under my table: as I have done, so hath God requited me. And they brought him to Jerusalem, and he died there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Juda besieging Jerusalem, took it, and put it to the sword, and set the whole city on fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And afterwards they went down and fought against the Chanaanite, who dwelt in the mountains, and in the south, and in the plains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda going forward against the Chanaanite, that dwelt in Hebron, (the name whereof was in former times Cariath-Arbe) slew Sesai, and Ahiman, and Tholmai:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And departing from thence, he went to the inhabitants of Dabir, the ancient name of which was Cariath-Sepher, that is, the city of letters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Caleb said: He that shall take Cariath-Sepher, and lay it waste, to him will I give my daughter Axa to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Othoniel, the son of Cenez, the younger brother of Caleb, having taken it, he gave him Axa his daughter to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as she was going on her way, her husband admonished her to ask a field of her father. And as she sighed sitting on her ass, Caleb said to her: What aileth thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But she answered: Give me a blessing, for thou hast given me a dry land: give me also a watery land So Caleb gave her the upper and the nether watery ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of the Cinite, the kinsman of Moses, went up from the city of palms, with the children of Juda, into the wilderness of his lot, which is at the south side of Arad, and they dwelt with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda went with Simeon, his brother, and they together defeated the Chanaanites that dwelt in Sephaath, and slew them. And the name of the city was called Horma, that is, Anathema.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Juda took Gaza, with its confines, and Ascalon, and Accaron, with their confines.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord was with Juda, and he possessed the hill country: but was not able to destroy the inhabitants of the valley, because they had many chariots armed with scythes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they gave Hebron to Caleb, as Moses had said, who destroyed out of it the three sons of Enac.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the sons of Benjamin did not destroy the Jebusites that inhabited Jerusalem: and the Jebusite hath dwelt with the sons of Benjamin in Jerusalem until this present day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The house of Joseph also went up against Bethel, and the Lord was with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when they were besieging the city, which before was called Luza,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They saw a man coming out of the city, and they said to him: Shew us the entrance into the city, and we will shew thee mercy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had shewed them, they smote the city with the edge of the sword: but that man, and all his kindred, they let go:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who being sent away, went into the land of Hetthim, and built there a city, and called it Luza: which is so called until this day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Manasses also did not destroy Bethsan, and Thanac, with their villages; nor the inhabitants of Dor, and Jeblaam, and Mageddo, with their villages. And the Chanaanite began to dwell with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after Israel was grown strong, he made them tributaries, and would not destroy them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Ephraim also did not slay the Chanaanite that dwelt in Gazer, but dwelt with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Zabulon destroyed not the inhabitants of Cetron, and Naalol: but the Chanaanite dwelt among them, and became their tributary.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Aser also destroyed not the inhabitants of Accho, and of Sidon, of Ahalab, and of Achazib, and of Helba, and of Aphec, and of Rohob:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he dwelt in the midst of the Chanaanites, the inhabitants of that land, and did not slay them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nephthali also destroyed not the inhabitants of Bethsames, and of Bethanath: and he dwelt in the midst of the Chanaanites, the inhabitants of the land, and the Bethsamites and Bethanites were tributaries to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Amorrhite straitened the children of Dan in the mountain, and gave them not a place to go down to the plain:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he dwelt in the mountain Hares, that is, of potsherds, in Aialon and Salebim. And the hand of the house of Joseph was heavy upon him, and he became tributary to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the border of the Amorrhite was from the ascent of the scorpion, the rock, and the higher places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And an angel of the Lord went up from Galgal to the place of weepers, and said: I made you go out of Egypt, and have brought you into the land for which I swore to your fathers: and I promised that I would not make void my covenant with you for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: On condition that you should not make a league with the inhabitants of this land, but should throw down their altars: and you would not hear my voice: why have you done this?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore I would not destroy them from before your face; that you may have enemies, and their gods may be your ruin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the angel of the Lord spoke these words to all the children of Israel: they lifted up their voice, and wept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the name of that place was called, The place of weepers, or of tears: and there they offered sacrifices to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Josue sent away the people, and the children of Israel went every one to his own possession to hold it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they served the Lord all his days, and the days of the ancients, that lived a long time after him, and who knew all the works of the Lord, which he had done for Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Josue, the son of Nun, the servant of the Lord, died, being a hundred and ten years old;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they buried him in the borders of his possession in Thamnathsare, in Mount Ephraim, on the north side of Mount Gaas.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all that generation was gathered to their fathers: and there arose others that knew not the Lord and the works which he had done for Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel did evil in the sight of the Lord, and they served Baalim,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they left the Lord, the God of their fathers, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt: and they followed strange gods, and the gods of the people that dwelt round about them, and they adored them: and they provoked the Lord to anger,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Forsaking him, and serving Baal and Astaroth

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord being angry against Israel, delivered them into the hands of plunderers: who took them and sold them to their enemies, that dwelt round about: neither could they stand against their enemies:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But whithersoever they meant to go, the hand of the Lord was upon them, as he had said, and as he had sworn to them: and they were greatly distressed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord raised up judges, to deliver them from the hands of those that oppressed them: but they would not hearken to them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Committing fornication with strange gods, and adoring them. They quickly forsook the way, in which their fathers had walked: and hearing the commandments of the Lord, they did all things contrary.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Lord raised them up judges, in their days, he was moved to mercy, and heard the groanings of the afflicted, and delivered them from the slaughter of the oppressors.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after the judge was dead, they returned, and did much worse things than their fathers had done, following strange gods, serving them, and adoring them. They left not their own inventions, and the stubborn way, by which they were accustomed to walk.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the wrath of the Lord was kindled against Israel, and he said: Behold this nation hath made void my covenant, which I had made with their fathers, and hath despised to hearken to my voice:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I also will not destroy the nations which Josue left when he died:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That through them I may try Israel, whether they will keep the way of the Lord, and walk in it, as their fathers kept it, or not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord therefore left all these nations, and would not quickly destroy them, neither did he deliver them into the hands of Josue.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These are the nations which the Lord left, that by them he might instruct Israel, and all that had not known the wars of the Chanaanites:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That afterwards their children might learn to fight with their enemies, and to be trained up to war:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The five princes of the Philistines, and all the Chanaanites, and the Sidonians, and the Hevites that dwelt in Mount Libanus, from Mount Baal Hermon to the entering into Emath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he left them, that he might try Israel by them, whether they would hear the commandments of the Lord, which he had commanded their fathers, by the hand of Moses, or not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the children of Israel dwelt in the midst of the Chanaanite, and the Hethite, and the Amorrhite, and the Pherezite, and the Hevite, and the Jebusite:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they took their daughters to wives, and they gave their own daughters to their sons, and they served their gods.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they did evil in the sight of the Lord, and they forgot their God, and served Baalim and Astaroth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord being angry with Israel, delivered them into the hands of Chusan Rasathaim, king of Mesopotamia, and they served him eight years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they cried to the Lord, who raised them up a saviour, and delivered them; to wit, Othoniel, the son of Cenez, the younger brother of Caleb:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the spirit of the Lord was in him, and he judged Israel. And he went out to fight, and the Lord delivered into his hands Chusan Rasathaim, king of Syria, and he overthrew him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the land rested forty years, and Othoniel, the son of Cenez, died.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the Lord: who strengthened against them Eglon, king of Moab: because they did evil in his sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he joined to him the children of Ammon, and Amalec: and he went and overthrew Israel, and possessed the city of palm trees.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel served Eglon, king of Moab, eighteen years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And afterwards they cried to the Lord, who raised them up a saviour, called Aod, the son of Gera, the son of Jemini, who used the left hand as well as the right. And the children of Israel sent presents to Eglon, king of Moab, by him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made himself a two-edged sword, with a haft in the midst of the length of the palm of the hand, and was girded therewith, under his garment, on the right thigh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he presented the gifts to Eglon, king of Moab Now Eglon was exceeding fat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had presented the gifts unto him he followed his companions that came along with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then returning from Galgal, where the idols were, he said to the king: I have a secret message to thee, O king. And he commanded silence: and all being gone out that were about him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Aod went in to him: now he was sitting in a summer parlour alone, and he said: I have a word from God to thee. And he forthwith rose up from his throne.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Aod put forth his left hand, and took the dagger from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With such force that the haft went in after the blade into the wound, and was closed up with the abundance of fat. So that he did not draw out the dagger, but left it in the body as he had struck it in: and forthwith, by the secret parts of nature, the excrements of the belly came out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Aod carefully shutting the doors of the parlour, and locking them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Went out by a postern door. And the king’s servants going in, saw the doors of the parlour shut, and they said: Perhaps he is easing nature in his summer parlour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And waiting a long time, till they were ashamed, and seeing that no man opened the door, they took a key: and opening, they found their lord lying dead on the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Aod, while they were in confusion, escaped, and passed by the place of the idols from whence he had returned. And he came to Seirath:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And forthwith he sounded the trumpet in Mount Ephraim: and the children of Israel went down with him, he himself going in the front.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Follow me: for the Lord hath delivered our enemies, the Moabites, into our hands. And they went down after him, and seized upon the fords of the Jordan, which are in the way to Moab: and they suffered no man to pass over:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they slew of the Moabites at that time, about ten thousand, all strong and valiant men: none of them could escape.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Moab was humbled that day under the hand of Israel: and the land rested eighty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him was Samgar, the son of Anath, who slew of the Philistines six hundred men with a ploughshare: and he also defended Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel again did evil in the sight of the Lord after the death of Aod:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord delivered them up into the hands of Jabin, king of Chanaan, who reigned in Asor: and he had a general of his army named Sisara, and he dwelt in Haroseth of the Gentiles.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel cried to the Lord: for he had nine hundred chariots set with scythes and for twenty years had grievously oppressed them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was at that time Debbora, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, who judged the people.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she sat under a palm tree, which was called by her name, between Rama and Bethel, in Mount Ephraim: and the children of Israel came up to her for all judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she sent and called Barac, the Son of Abinoem, out of Cedes, in Nephthali: and she said to him: The Lord God of Israel hath commanded thee: Go, and lead an army to Mount Thabor, and thou shalt take with thee ten thousand fighting men of the children of Nephthali, and of the children of Zabulon:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I will bring unto thee in the place of the torrent Cison, Sisara, the general of Jabin’s army, and his chariots, and all his multitude, and will deliver them into thy hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Barac said to her: If thou wilt come with me, I will go: if thou wilt not come with me, I will not go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She said to him: I will go, indeed, with thee, but at this time the victory shall not be attributed to thee, because Sisara shall be delivered into the hand of a woman. Debbora therefore arose, and went with Barac to Cedes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he called unto him Zabulon and Nephthali, and went up with ten thousand fighting men, having Debbora in his company.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Haber, the Cinite, had some time before departed from the rest of the Cinites, his brethren, the sons of Hobab, the kinsman of Moses: and had pitched his tents unto the valley, which is called Sennim, and was near Cedes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was told Sisara, that Barac, the son of Abinoem, was gone up to Mount Thabor:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he gathered together his nine hundred chariots armed with scythes, and all his army, from Haroseth of the Gentiles, to the torrent Cison.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Debbora said to Barac: Arise, for this is the day wherein the Lord hath delivered Sisara into thy hands: behold, he is thy leader. And Barac went down from Mount Thabor, and ten thousand fighting men with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord struck a terror into Sisara, and all his chariots, and all his multitude, with the edge of the sword, at the sight of Barac; insomuch, that Sisara leaping down from off his chariot, fled away on foot,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Barac pursued after the fleeing chariots, and the army, unto Haroseth of the Gentiles; and all the multitude of the enemies was utterly destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Sisara fleeing, came to the tent of Jahel, the wife of Haber, the Cinite, for there was peace between Jabin, the king of Asor, and the house of Haber, the Cinite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jahel went forth to meet Sisara, and said to him: Come in to me, my lord; come in, fear not. He went into her tent, and being covered by her with a cloak,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Said to her: Give me, I beseech thee, a little water, for I am very thirsty. She opened a bottle of milk, and gave him to drink, and covered him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Sisara said to her: Stand before the door of the tent, and when any shall come and inquire of thee, saying: Is there any man here? thou shalt say: There is none.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Jahel, Haber’s wife, took a nail of the tent, and taking also a hammer: and going in softly, and with silence, she put the nail upon the temples of his head, and striking it with the hammer, drove it through his brain fast into the ground: and so passing from deep sleep to death, he fainted away and died.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold, Barac came pursuing after Sisara: and Jahel went out to meet him, and said to him: Come, and I will shew thee the man whom thou seekest. And when he came into her tent, he saw Sisara lying dead, and the nail fastened in his temples.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So God that day humbled Jabin, the king of Chanaan, before the children of Israel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who grew daily stronger, and with a mighty hand overpowered Jabin, king of Chanaan, till they quite destroyed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In that day Debbora and Barac, son of Abinoem, sung, and said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O you of Israel, that have willingly offered your lives to danger, bless the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hear, O ye kings, give ear, O ye princes: It is I, it is I, that will sing to the Lord, I will sing to the Lord, the God of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Lord, when thou wentest out of Seir, and passedst by the regions of Edom, the earth trembled, and the heavens and clouds dropped water.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mountains melted before the face of the Lord, and Sinai before the face of the Lord the God of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the days of Samgar, the son of Anath, in the days of Jahel, the paths rested: and they that went by them, walked through bye-ways.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The valiant men ceased, and rested in Israel: until Debbora arose, a mother arose in Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord chose new wars, and he himself overthrew the gates of the enemies: a shield and spear was not seen among forty thousand of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My heart loveth the princes of Israel: O you, that of your own good will offered yourselves to danger, bless the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak, you that ride upon fair asses, and you that sit in judgment, and walk in the way.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where the chariots were dashed together, and the army of the enemies was choked, there let the justices of the Lord be rehearsed, and his clemency towards the brave men of Israel: then the people of the Lord went down to the gates, and obtained the sovereignty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arise, arise, O Debbora, arise, arise, and utter a canticle. Arise, Barac, and take hold of thy captives, O son of Abinoem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The remnants of the people are saved, the Lord hath fought among the valiant ones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Out of Ephraim he destroyed them into Amalec, and after him out of Benjamin into thy people, O Amalec: Out of Machir there came down princes, and out of Zabulon they that led the army to fight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The captains of Issachar were with Debbora, and followed the steps of Barac, who exposed himself to danger, as one going headlong, and into a pit. Ruben being divided against himself, there was found a strife of courageous men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Why dwellest thou between two borders, that thou mayst hear the bleatings of the flocks? Ruben being divided against himself, there was found a strife of courageous men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Galaad rested beyond the Jordan, and Dan applied himself to ships: Aser dwelt on the sea shore, and abode in the havens.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Zabulon and Nephthali offered their lives to death in the region of Merome.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The kings came and fought, the kings of Chanaan fought in Thanac, by the waters of Mageddo and yet they took no spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was war made against them from heaven: the stars, remaining in their order and courses, fought against Sisara.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The torrent of Cison dragged their carcasses, the torrent of Cadumim, the torrent of Cison: tread thou, my soul, upon the strong ones.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The hoofs of the horses were broken whilst the stoutest of the enemies fled amain, and fell headlong down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Curse ye the land of Meroz, said the angel of the Lord: curse the inhabitants thereof, because they came not to the help of the Lord, to help his most valiant men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed among women be Jahel, the wife of Haber the Cinite, and blessed be she in her tent.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He asked her water, and she gave him milk, and offered him butter in a dish fit for princes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She put her left hand to the nail, and her right hand to the workman’s hammer, and she struck Sisara, seeking in his head a place for the wound, and strongly piercing through his temples.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Between her feet he fell: he fainted, and he died: he rolled before her feet, and there he lay lifeless and wretched.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His mother looked out at a window, and howled: and she spoke from the dining room: Why is his chariot so long in coming back? Why are the feet of his horses so slow?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One that was wiser than the rest of his wives, returned this answer to her mother in law:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Perhaps he is now dividing the spoils, and the fairest of the women is chosen out for him: garments of divers colours are given to Sisara for his prey, and furniture of different kinds is heaped together to adorn necks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So let all thy enemies perish, O Lord: but let them that love thee shine, as the sun shineth in his rising.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the land rested for forty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel again did evil in the sight of the Lord: and he delivered them into the hand of Madian seven years,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they were grievously oppressed by them. And they made themselves dens and caves in the mountains, and strong holds to resist.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Israel had sown, Madian and Amalec, and the rest of the eastern nations, came up:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And pitching their tents among them, wasted all things as they were in the blade, even to the entrance of Gaza: and they left nothing at all in Israel for sustenance of life, nor sheep, nor oxen, nor asses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they and all their flocks came with their tents, and like locusts filled all places, an innumerable multitude of men, and of camels, wasting whatsoever they touched.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Israel was humbled exceedingly in the sight of Madian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he cried to the Lord, desiring help against the Madianites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent unto them a prophet, and he spoke: Thus saith the Lord, the God of Israel: I made you to come up out of Egypt, and brought you out of the house of bondage,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And delivered you out of the hands of the Egyptians, and of all the enemies that afflicted you: and I cast them out at your coming in, and gave you their land.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I said: I am the Lord your God, fear not the gods of the Amorrhites, in whose land you dwell. And you would not hear my voice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And an angel of the Lord came, and sat under an oak that was in Ephra, and belonged to Joas, the father of the family of Ezri. And when Gedeon, his son, was threshing and cleansing wheat by the winepress, to flee from Madian,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The angel of the Lord appeared to him, and said: The Lord is with thee, O most valiant of men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon said to him: I beseech thee, my lord, if the Lord be with us, why have these evils fallen upon us? Where are his miracles, which our fathers have told us of, saying: The Lord brought us out of Egypt but now the Lord hath forsaken us, and delivered us into the hand of Madian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord looked upon him, and said: Go, in this thy strength, and thou shalt deliver Israel out of the hand of Madian: know that I have sent thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered, and said: I beseech thee, my lord wherewith shall I deliver Israel? Behold, my family is the meanest in Manasses, and I am the least in my father’s house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him: I will be with thee: and thou shalt cut off Madian as one man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: If I have found grace before thee, give me a sign that it is thou that speakest to me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And depart not hence, till I return to thee, and bring a sacrifice, and offer it to thee. And he answered: I will wait thy coming.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Gedeon went in, and boiled a kid, and made unleavened loaves of a measure of flour: and putting the flesh in a basket, and the broth of the flesh into a pot, he carried all under the oak, and presented to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the angel of the Lord said to him: Take the flesh and the unleavened loaves, and lay them upon that rock, and pour out the broth thereon. And when he had done so,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The angel of the Lord put forth the tip of the rod, which he held in his hand, and touched the flesh and the unleavened loaves: and there arose a fire from the rock, and consumed the flesh and the unleavened loaves: and the angel of the Lord vanished out of his sight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon seeing that it was the angel of the Lord, said: Alas, my Lord God: for I have seen the angel of the Lord face to face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to him: Peace be with thee: fear not, thou shalt not die.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon built there an altar to the Lord, and called it the Lord’s peace, until this present day. And when he was yet in Ephra, which is of the family of Ezri,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That night the Lord said to him: Take a bullock of thy father’s, and another bullock of seven years, and thou shalt destroy the altar of Baal, which is thy father’s: and cut down the grove that is about the altar:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou shalt build an altar to the Lord thy God, in the top of this rock, whereupon thou didst lay the sacrifice before: and thou shalt take the second bullock, and shalt offer a holocaust upon a pile of the wood, which thou shalt cut down out of the grove.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Gedeon, taking ten men of his servants, did as the Lord had commanded him. But fearing his father’s house, and the men of that city, he would not do it by day, but did all by night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the men of that town were risen in the morning, they saw the altar of Baal destroyed, and the grove cut down, and the second bullock laid upon the altar, which then was built.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said one to another: Who hath done this? And when they inquired for the author of the fact, it was said: Gedeon, the son of Joas, did all this.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to Joas: Bring out thy son hither, that he may die: because he hath destroyed the altar of Baal, and hath cut down his grove.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered them: Are you the avengers of Baal, that you fight for him? he that is his adversary, let him die before to morrow light appear: if he be a god, let him revenge himself on him that hath cast down his altar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From that day Gedeon was called Jerobaal, because Joas had said: Let Baal revenge himself on him that hath cast down his altar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now all Madian, and Amalec, and the eastern people, were gathered together, and passing over the Jordan, camped in the valley of Jezrael.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the spirit of the Lord came upon Gedeon, and he sounded the trumpet, and called together the house of Abiezer, to follow him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent messengers into all Manasses, and they also followed him: and other messengers into Aser and Zabulon, and Nephthali, and they came to meet him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon said to God: If thou wilt save Israel by my hand, as thou hast said,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will put this fleece of wool on the floor: if there be dew on the fleece only, and it be dry on all the ground beside, I shall know that by my hand, as thou hast said, thou wilt deliver Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was so. And rising before day, wringing the fleece, he filled a vessel with the dew.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said again to God: Let not thy wrath be kindled against me, if I try once more, seeking a sign in the fleece. I pray that the fleece only may be dry, and all the ground wet with dew.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God did that night as he had requested: and it was dry on the fleece only, and there was dew on all the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Jerobaal, who is the same as Gedeon, rising up early, and all the people with him, came to the fountain that is called Harad. Now the camp of Madian was in the valley, on the north side of the high hill.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Gedeon: The people that are with thee are many, and Madian shall not be delivered into their hands: lest Israel should glory against me, and say: I was delivered by my own strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak to the people, and proclaim in the hearing of all: Whosoever is fearful and timorous, let him return. So two and twenty thousand men went away from Mount Galaad and returned home, and only ten thousand remained.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Gedeon: The people are still too many, bring them to the waters, and there I will try them: and of whom I shall say to thee, This shall go with thee, let him go: whom I shall forbid to go, let him return.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the people were come down to the waters, the Lord said to Gedeon: They that shall lap the water with their tongues, as dogs are wont to lap, thou shalt set apart by themselves: but they that shall drink bowing down their knees, shall be on the other side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the number of them that had lapped water; casting it with the hand to their mouth, was three hundred men: and all the rest of the multitude had drunk kneeling.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to Gedeon: By the three hundred men, that lapped water, I will save you, and deliver Madian into thy hand: but let all the rest of the people return to their place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So taking victuals and trumpets according to their number, he ordered all the rest of the multitude to depart to their tents: and he with the three hundred gave himself to the battle. Now the camp of Madia was beneath him in the valley.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The same night the Lord said to him: Arise, and go down into the camp: because I have delivered them into thy hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if thou be afraid to go alone, let Phara, thy servant, go down with thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when thou shalt hear what they are saying, then shall thy hands be strengthened, and thou shalt go down more secure to the enemies’ camp. And he went down with Phara his servant, into part of the camp, where was the watch of men in arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Madian and Amalec, and all the eastern people, lay scattered in the valley, as a multitude of locusts: their camels also were innumerable, as the sand that lieth on the sea shore.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Gedeon was come, one told his neighbour a dream: and in this manner related what he had seen: I dreamt a dream, and it seemed to me as if a hearth cake of barley bread rolled and came down into the camp of Madian: and when it was come to a tent, it struck it, and beat it down flat to the ground.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He to whom he spoke, answered: This is nothing else but the sword of Gedeon, the son of Joas, a man of Israel. For the Lord hath delivered Madian, and all their camp into his hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Gedeon had heard the dream, and the interpretation thereof, he adored: and returned to the camp of Israel, and said: Arise, for the Lord hath delivered the camp of Madian into our hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he divided the three hundred men into three parts, and gave them trumpets in their hands, and empty pitchers, and lamps within the pitchers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: What you shall see me do, do you the same: I will go into one part of the camp, and do you as I shall do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the trumpet shall sound in my hand, do you also blow the trumpets on every side of the camp, and shout together to the Lord and to Gedeon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon, and the three hundred men that were with him, went into part of the camp, at the beginning of the midnight watch, and the watchmen being alarmed, they began to sound their trumpets, and to clap the pitchers one against another.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they sounded their trumpets in three places round about the camp, and had broken their pitchers, they held their lamps in their left hands, and with their right hands the trumpets which they blew, and they cried out: The sword of the Lord and of Gedeon:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Standing every man in his place round about the enemies’ camp. So all the camp was troubled, and crying out and howling, they fled away:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the three hundred men nevertheless persisted sounding the trumpets. And the Lord sent the sword into all the camp, and they killed one another,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Fleeing as far as Bethsetta, and the border of Abelmahula, in Tebbath. But the men of Israel, shouting from Nephthali, and Aser, and from all Manasses, pursued after Madian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon sent messengers into all Mount Ephraim, saying: Come down to meet Madian, and take the waters before them to Bethbera and the Jordan. And all Ephraim shouted, and took the waters before them and the Jordan as far as Bethbera.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And having taken two men of Madian, Oreb and Zeb: Oreb they slew in the rock of Oreb, and Zeb in the winepress of Zeb. And they pursued Madian, carrying the heads of Oreb and Zeb to Gedeon, beyond the waters of the Jordan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the men of Ephraim said to him: What is this that thou meanest to do, that thou wouldst not call us, when thou wentest to fight against Madian? And they chid him sharply, and almost offered violence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: What could I have done like to that which you have done? Is not one bunch of grapes of Ephraim better than the vintages of Abiezer?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Lord hath delivered into your hands the princes of Madian, Oreb and Zeb: what could I have done like to what you have done? And when he had said this, their spirit was appeased, with which they swelled against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Gedeon was come to the Jordan, he passed over it with the three hundred men that were with him: who were so weary that they could not pursue after them that fled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to the men of Soccoth: Give, I beseech you, bread to the people that is with me, for they are faint: that we may pursue Zebee, and Salmana, the kings of Madian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The princes of Soccoth answered: Peradventure the palms of the hands of Zebee and Salmana are in thy hand, and therefore thou demandest that we should give bread to thy army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: When the Lord therefore shall have delivered Zebee and Salmana into my hands, I will thresh your flesh with the thorns and briers of the desert.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going up from thence, he came to Phanuel: and he spoke the like things to the men of that place. And they also answered him, as the men of Soccoth had answered.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said, therefore, to them also: When I shall return a conqueror in peace, I will destroy this tower.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Zebee and Salmana were resting with all their army. For fifteen thousand men were left of all the troops of the eastern people, and one hundred and twenty thousand warriors that drew the sword were slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon went up by the way of them that dwelt in tents, on the east of Nobe and Jegbaa, and smote the camp of the enemies, who were secure, and suspected no hurt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Zebee and Salmana fled, and Gedeon pursued and took them, all their host being put in confusion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And returning from the battle before the sun rising,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He took a boy of the men of Soccoth: and he asked him the names of the princes and ancients of Soccoth, and he described unto him seventy-seven men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came to Soccoth, and said to them: Behold Zebee, and Salmana, concerning whom you upbraided me, saying: Peradventure the hands of Zebee and Salmana are in thy hands, and therefore thou demandest that we should give bread to the men that are weary and faint.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he took the ancients of the city, and thorns and briers of the desert, and tore them with the same, and cut in pieces the men of Soccoth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he demolished the tower of Phanuel, and slew the men of the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to Zebee and Salmana: What manner of men were they, whom you slew in Thabor? They answered: They were like thee, and one of them as the son of a king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered them: They were my brethren, the sons of my mother. As the Lord liveth, if you had saved them, I would not kill you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to Jether, his eldest son: Arise, and slay them. But he drew not his sword: for he was afraid, being but yet a boy.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Zebee and Salmana said: Do thou rise and run upon us: because the strength of a man is according to his age: Gedeon rose up, and slew Zebee and Salmana: and he took the ornaments and bosses, with which the necks of the camels of kings are wont to be adorned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the men of Israel said to Gedeon: Rule thou over us, and thy son, and thy son’s son: because thou hast delivered us from the hand of Madian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: I will not rule over you, neither shall my son rule over you, but the Lord shall rule over you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: I desire one request of you: Give me the earlets of your spoils. For the Ismaelites were accustomed to wear golden earlets.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered: We will give them most willingly. And spreading a mantle on the ground, they cast upon it the earlets of the spoils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the weight of the earlets that he requested, was a thousand seven hundred sicles of gold, besides the ornaments, and jewels, and purple raiment, which the kings of Madian were wont to use, and besides the golden chains that were about the camels necks.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon made an ephod thereof, and put it in his city Ephra. And all Israel committed fornication with it, and it became a ruin to Gedeon, and to all his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Madian was humbled before the children of Israel, neither could they any more lift up their heads: but the land rested for forty years, while Gedeon presided.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Jerobaal, the son of Joas, went and dwelt in his own house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he had seventy sons, who came out of his thigh, for he had many wives.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his concubine, that he had in Sichem, bore him a son, whose name was Abimelech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gedeon, the son of Joas died in a good old age, and was buried in the sepulchre of his father, in Ephra, of the family of Ezri.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But after Gedeon was dead, the children of Israel turned again, and committed fornication with Baalim. And they made a covenant with Baal, that he should be their god:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they remembered not the Lord their God, who delivered them out of the hands of all their enemies round about:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither did they shew mercy to the house of Jerobaal Gedeon, according to all the good things he had done to Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abimelech, the son of Jerobaal, went to Sichem, to his mother’s brethren, and spoke to them, and to all the kindred of his mother’s father, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak to all the men of Sichem: whether is better for you that seventy men, all the sons of Jerobaal, should rule over you, or that one man should rule over you? And withal, consider that I am your bone, and your flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his mother’s brethren spoke of him to all the men of Sichem, all these words, and they inclined their hearts after Abimelech, saying: He is our brother:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they gave him seventy weight of silver out of the temple of Baalberith: wherewith he hired to himself men that were needy, and vagabonds, and they followed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came to his father’s house in Ephra, and slew his brethren, the sons of Jerobaal, seventy men, upon one stone: and there remained only Joatham, the youngest son of Jerobaal, who was hidden.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the men of Sichem were gathered together, and all the families of the city of Mello: and they went and made Abimelech king, by the oak that stood in Sichem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This being told to Joatham, he went, and stood on the top of Mount Garizim: and lifting up his voice, he cried, and said: Hear me, ye men of Sichem, so may God hear you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The trees went to anoint a king over them: and they said to the olive tree: Reign thou over us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it answered: Can I leave my fatness, which both gods and men make use of, to come to be promoted among the trees?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the trees said to the fig tree: Come thou and reign over us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it answered them: Can I leave my sweetness, and my delicious fruits, and go to be promoted among the other trees?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the trees said to the vine: Come thou and reign over us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it answered them: Can I forsake my wine, that cheereth God and men, and be promoted among the other trees?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the trees said to the bramble: Come thou and reign over us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it answered them: If, indeed, you mean to make me king, come ye, and rest under my shadow: but if you mean it not, let fire come out from the bramble, and devour the cedars of Libanus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, if you have done well, and without sin, in appointing Abimelech king over you, and have dealt well with Jerobaal, and with his house, and have made a suitable return for the benefits of him who fought for you,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And exposed his life to dangers, to deliver you from the hand of Madian,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And you are now risen up against my father’s house, and have killed his sons, seventy men, upon one stone, and have made Abimelech, the son of his handmaid, king over the inhabitants of Sichem, because he is your brother:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If therefore you have dealt well, and without fault, with Jerobaal and his house, rejoice ye, this day, in Abimelech, and may he rejoice in you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if unjustly: let fire come out from him, and consume the inhabitants of Sichem, and the town of Mello: and let fire come out from the men of Sichem and from the town of Mello, and devour Abimelech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had said thus, he fled, and went into Bera: and dwelt there for fear of Abimelech, his brother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Abimelech reigned over Israel three years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord sent a very evil spirit between Abimelech and the inhabitants of Sichem; who began to detest him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to lay the crime of the murder of the seventy sons of Jerobaal, and the shedding of their blood, upon Abimelech, their brother, and upon the rest of the princes of the Sichemites, who aided him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they set an ambush against him on the top of the mountains: and while they waited for his coming, they committed robberies, taking spoils of all that passed by: and it was told Abimelech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gaal, the son of Obed, came with his brethren, and went over to Sichem. And the inhabitants of Sichem, taking courage at his coming,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Went out into the fields, wasting the vineyards, and treading down the grapes: and singing and dancing, they went into the temple of their god, and in their banquets and cups they cursed Abimelech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gaal, the son of Obed, cried: Who is Abimelech, and what is Sichem, that we should serve him? Is he not the son of Jerobaal, and hath made Zebul, his servant, ruler over the men of Emor, the father of Sichem? Why then shall we serve him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Would to God that some man would put this people under my hand, that I might remove Abimelech out of the way. And it was said to Abimelech: Gather together the multitude of an army, and come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For Zebul, the ruler of the city, hearing the words of Gaal, the son of Obed, was very angry,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And sent messengers privately to Abimelech, saying: Behold, Gaal, the son of Obed, is come into Sichem with his brethren, and endeavoureth to set the city against thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arise, therefore, in the night, with the people that is with thee, and lie hid in the field:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And betimes in the morning, at sun rising, set upon the city, and when he shall come out against thee, with his people, do to him what thou shalt be able.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Abimelech, therefore, arose with all his army, by night, and laid ambushes near Sichem in four places.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Gaal, the son of Obed, went out, and stood in the entrance of the gate of the city. And Abimelech rose up, and all his army with him, from the places of the ambushes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Gaal saw the people, he said to Zebul: Behold, a multitude cometh down from the mountains. And he answered him: Thou seest the shadows of the mountains as if they were the heads of men, and this is thy mistake.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again Gaal said: Behold, there cometh people down from the midst of the land, and one troop cometh by the way that looketh towards the oak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Zebul said to him: Where is now thy mouth, wherewith thou saidst: Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him? Is not this the people which thou didst despise? Go out, and fight against him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Gaal went out, in the sight of the people of Sichem, and fought against Abimelech,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who chased and put him to flight, and drove him to the city: and many were slain of his people, even to the gate of the city:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abimelech sat down in Ruma: but Zebul drove Gaal, and his companions, out of the city, and would not suffer them to abide in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the day following the people went out into the field. And it was told to Abimelech,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he took his army, and divided it into three companies, and laid ambushes in the fields. And seeing that the people came out of the city, he arose, and set upon them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: With his own company, assaulting and besieging the city: whilst the two other companies chased the enemies that were scattered about the field.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abimelech assaulted the city all that day: and took it, and killed the inhabitants thereof, and demolished it, so that he sowed salt in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they who dwelt in the tower of Sichem, had heard this, they went into the temple of their god Berith, where they had made a covenant with him, and from thence the place had taken its name, and it was exceeding strong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Abimelech also hearing that the men of the tower of Sichem were gathered together,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Went up into mount Selmon, he and all his people with him: and taking an axe, he cut down the bough of a tree, and laying it on his shoulder, and carrying it, he said to his companions: What you see me do, do ye out of hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they cut down boughs from the trees, every man as fast as he could, and followed their leader. And surrounding the fort, they set it on fire: and so it came to pass, that with the smoke and with the fire a thousand persons were killed, men and women together, of the inhabitants of the town of Sichem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Abimelech, departing from thence, came to the town of Thebes, which he surrounded and besieged with his army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there was in the midst of the city a high tower, to which both the men and the women were fled together, and all the princes of the city, and having shut and strongly barred the gate, they stood upon the battlements of the tower to defend themselves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Abimelech, coming near the tower, fought stoutly: and, approaching to the gate, endeavoured to set fire to it:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold, a certain woman casting a piece of a millstone from above, dashed it against the head of Abimelech, and broke his skull.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he called hastily to his armourbearer, and said to him: Draw thy sword, and kill me: lest it should be said that I was slain by a woman. He did as he was commanded, and slew him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was dead all the men of Israel that were with him, returned to their homes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God repaid the evil that Abimelech had done against his father, killing his seventy brethren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Sichemites also were rewarded for what they had done, and the curse of Joatham, the son of Jerobaal, came upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After Abimelech, there arose a ruler in Israel, Thola, son of Phua, the uncle of Abimelech, a man of Issachar, who dwelt in Samir of mount Ephraim:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he judged Israel three and twenty years, and he died, and was buried in Samir.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To him succeeded Jair, the Galaadite, who judged Israel for two and twenty years,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Having thirty sons, that rode on thirty ass colts, and were princes of thirty cities, which from his name were called Havoth Jair, that is, the towns of Jair, until this present day, in the land of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jair died, and was buried in the place which is called Camon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the children of Israel, adding new sins to their old ones, did evil in the sight of the Lord, and served idols, Baalim and Astaroth, and the gods of Syria, and of Sidon, and of Moab, and of the children of Ammon, and of the Philistines: and they left the Lord, and did not serve him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord being angry with them, delivered them into the hands of the Philistines, and of the children of Ammon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they were afflicted, and grievously oppressed for eighteen years, all they that dwelt beyond the Jordan in the land of the Amorrhite, who is in Galaad:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Insomuch that the children of Ammon, passing over the Jordan, wasted Juda, and Benjamin, and Ephraim: and Israel was distressed exceedingly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they cried to the Lord, and said, We have sinned against thee, because we have forsaken the Lord our God, and have served Baalim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord said to them: Did not the Egyptians, and the Amorrhites, and the children of Ammon, and the Philistines,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The Sidonians also, and Amalec, and Chanaan, oppress you, and you cried to me, and I delivered you out of their hand?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And yet you have forsaken me, and have worshipped strange gods: therefore I will deliver you no more:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go, and call upon the gods which you have chosen: let them deliver you in the time of distress.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel said to the Lord: We have sinned, do thou unto us whatsoever pleaseth thee: only deliver us this time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And saying these things, they cast away out of their coasts all the idols of strange gods, and served the Lord their God: and he was touched with their miseries.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Ammon shouting together, pitched their tents in Galaad: against whom the children of Israel assembled themselves together, and camped in Maspha.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the princes of Galaad said one to another: Whosoever of us shall first begin to fight against the children of Ammon, he shall be the leader of the people of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was at that time Jephte, the Galaadite, a most valiant man, and a warrior, the son of a woman that was a harlot, and his father was Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Galaad had a wife of whom he had sons: who, after they were grown up, thrust out Jephte, saying: Thou canst not inherit in the house of our father, because thou art born of another mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then he fled and avoided them, and dwelt in the land of Tob: and there were gathered to him needy men and robbers, and they followed him as their prince.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days the children of Ammon made war against Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as they pressed hard upon them, the ancients of Galaad went to fetch Jephte out of the land of Tob to help them:
