source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to him: Come thou, and be our prince, and fight against the children of Ammon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: Are not you the men that hated me, and cast me out of my father’s house, and now you are come to me, constrained by necessity?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the princes of Galaad said to Jephte: For this cause we are now come to thee, that thou mayst go with us, and fight against the children of Ammon, and be head over all the inhabitants of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jephte also said to them: If you be come to me sincerely, that I should fight for you against the children of Ammon, and the Lord shall deliver them into my hand, shall I be your prince?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered him: The Lord, who heareth these things, he himself is mediator and witness that we will do as we have promised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Jephte therefore went with the princes of Galaad, and all the people made him their prince. And Jephte spoke all his words before the Lord in Maspha.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent messengers to the king of the children of Ammon, to say in his name: What hast thou to do with me, that thou art come against me, to waste my land?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: Because Israel took away my land, when he came up out of Egypt, from the confines of the Arnon unto the Jaboc and the Jordan: now, therefore, restore the same peaceably to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jephte again sent word by them, and commanded them to say to the king of Ammon:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus saith Jephte: Israel did not take away the land of Moab, nor the land of the children of Ammon:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when they came up out of Egypt, he walked through the desert to the Red Sea, and came into Cades.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying: Suffer me to pass through thy land. But he would not condescend to his request. He sent also to the king of Moab, who, likewise, refused to give him passage. He abode, therefore, in Cades,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And went round the land of Edom at the side, and the land of Moab: and came over against the east coast of the land of Moab, and camped on the other side of the Arnon: and he would not enter the bounds of Moab.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Israel sent messengers to Sehon, king of the Amorrhites, who dwelt in Hesebon, and they said to him: Suffer me to pass through thy land to the river.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he, also despising the words of Israel, suffered him not to pass through his borders: but gathering an infinite multitude, went out against him to Jasa, and made strong opposition.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord delivered him, with all his army, into the hands of Israel, and he slew him, and possessed all the land of the Amorrhite, the inhabitant of that country,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the coasts thereof from the Arnon to the Jaboc, and from the wilderness to the Jordan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the Lord, the God of Israel, destroyed the Amorrhite, his people of Israel fighting against him, and wilt thou now possess his land?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Are not those things which thy god Chamos possesseth, due to thee by right? But what the Lord our God hath obtained by conquest, shall be our possession:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Unless, perhaps, thou art better than Balac, the son of Sephor, king of Moab: or canst shew that he strove against Israel, and fought against him,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whereas he hath dwelt in Hesebon, and the villages thereof, and in Aroer, and its villages, and in all the cities near the Jordan, for three hundred years. Why have you for so long a time attempted nothing about this claim?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I do not trespass against thee, but thou wrongest me by declaring an unjust war against me. The Lord be judge, and decide this day, between Israel and the children of Ammon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king of the children of Ammon would not hearken to the words of Jephte, which he sent him by the messengers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore the spirit of the Lord came upon Jephte, and going round Galaad, and Manasses, and Maspha of Galaad, and passing over from thence to the children of Ammon,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He made a vow to the Lord, saying: If thou wilt deliver the children of Ammon into my hands,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whosoever shall first come forth out of the doors of my house, and shall meet me, when I return in peace from the children of Ammon, the same will I offer a holocaust to the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jephte passed over to the children of Ammon to fight against them: and the Lord delivered them into his hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he smote them from Aroer till you come to Mennith, twenty cities, and as far as Abel, which is set with vineyards, with a very great slaughter: and the children of Ammon were humbled by the children of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Jephte returned into Maspha, to his house, his only daughter met him with timbrels and with dances: for he had no other children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he saw her, he rent his garments, and said: Alas! my daughter, thou hast deceived me, and thou thyself art deceived: for I have opened my mouth to the Lord, and I can do no other thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she answered him: My father, if thou hast opened thy mouth to the Lord, do unto me whatsoever thou hast promised, since the victory hath been granted to thee, and revenge of thy enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she said to her father: Grant me only this, which I desire: Let me go, that I may go about the mountains for two months, and may bewail my virginity with my companions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered her: Go. And he sent her away for two months. And when she was gone with her comrades and companions, she mourned her virginity in the mountains.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the two months being expired, she returned to her father, and he did to her as he had vowed, and she knew no man. From thence came a fashion in Israel, and a custom has been kept:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That, from year to year, the daughters of Israel assemble together, and lament the daughter of Jephte the Galaadite, for four days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But behold there arose a sedition in Ephraim. And passing towards the north, they said to Jephte: When thou wentest to fight against the children of Ammon, why wouldst thou not call us, that we might go with thee? Therefore we will burn thy house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered them: I and my people were at great strife with the children of Ammon: and I called you to assist me, and you would not do it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when I saw this, I put my life in my own hands, and passed over against the children of Ammon and the Lord delivered them into my hands. What have I deserved, that you should rise up to fight against me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then calling to him all the men of Galaad, he fought against Ephraim: and the men of Galaad defeated Ephraim, because he had said: Galaad is a fugitive of Ephraim, and dwelleth in the midst of Ephraim and Manasses.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Galaadites secured the fords of the Jordan, by which Ephraim was to return. And when any one of the number of Ephraim came thither in the flight, and said: I beseech you let me pass: the Galaadites said to him: Art thou not an Ephraimite? If he said: I am not:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They asked him: Say then, Scibboleth, which is interpreted, An ear of corn. But he answered, Sibboleth, not being able to express an ear of corn by the same letter. Then presently they took him and killed him in the very passage of the Jordan. And there fell at that time of Ephraim, two and forty thousand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Jephte, the Galaadite, judged Israel six years: and he died, and was buried in his city of Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him Abesan of Bethlehem judged Israel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He had thirty sons, and as many daughters, whom he sent abroad, and gave to husbands, and took wives for his sons, of the same number, bringing them into his house. And he judged Israel seven years:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he died, and was buried in Bethlehem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To him succeeded Ahialon, a Zabulonite: and he judged Israel ten years:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he died, and was buried in Zabulon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After him, Abdon, the son of Illel, a Pharathonite, judged Israel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he had forty sons, and of them thirty grandsons, mounted upon seventy ass colts, and he judged Israel eight years:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he died, and was buried in Pharathon, in the land of Ephraim, in the mount of Amalech.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the Lord: and he delivered them into the hands of the Philistines forty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there was a certain man of Saraa, and of the race of Dan, whose name was Manue, and his wife was barren.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And an angel of the Lord appeared to her, and said: Thou art barren and without children: but thou shalt conceive and bear a son.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now therefore beware, and drink no wine nor strong drink, and eat not any unclean thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because thou shalt conceive, and bear a son, and no razor shall touch his head: for he shall be a Nazarite of God, from his infancy, and from his mother’s womb, and he shall begin to deliver Israel from the hands of the Philistines.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when she was come to her husband, she said to him: A man of God came to me, having the countenance of an angel, very awful. And when I asked him who he was, and whence he came, and by what name he was called, he would not tell me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he answered thus: Behold thou shalt conceive and bear a son: beware thou drink no wine, nor strong drink, nor eat any unclean thing: for the child shall be a Nazarite of God from his infancy, from his mother’s womb until the day of his death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Manue prayed to the Lord, and said: I beseech thee, O Lord, that the man of God, whom thou didst send, may come again, and teach us what we ought to do concerning the child, that shall be born.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord heard the prayer of Manue, and the angel of the Lord appeared again to his wife, as she was sitting in the field. But Manue her husband was not with her. And when she saw the angel,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: She made haste, and ran to her husband: and told him, saying: Behold the man hath appeared to me, whom I saw before.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He rose up, and followed his wife: and coming to the man, said to him: Art thou he that spoke to the woman? And he answered: I am.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Manue said to him: When thy word shall come to pass, what wilt thou that the child should do? or from what shall he keep himself?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the angel of the Lord said to Manue: From all the things I have spoken of to thy wife, let her refrain herself:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let her eat nothing that cometh of the vine, neither let her drink wine or strong drink, nor eat any unclean thing: and whatsoever I have commanded her, let her fulfil and observe.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Manue said to the angel of the Lord: I beseech thee to consent to my request, and let us dress a kid for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the angel answered him: If thou press me I will not eat of thy bread: but if thou wilt offer a holocaust, offer it to the Lord. And Manue knew not it was the angel of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to him: What is thy name, that, if thy word shall come to pass, we may honour thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered him: Why askest thou my name, which is wonderful?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Manue took a kid of the flocks, and the libations, and put them upon a rock, offering to the Lord, who doth wonderful things: and he and his wife looked on.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the flame from the altar went up towards heaven, the angel of the Lord ascended also in the flame. And when Manue and his wife saw this, they fell flat on the ground;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the angel of the Lord appeared to them no more. And forthwith Manue understood that it was an angel of the Lord,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to his wife: We shall certainly die, because we have seen God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his wife answered him: If the Lord had a mind to kill us, he would not have received a holocaust and libations at our hands; neither would he have shewed us all these things, nor have told us the things that are to come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she bore a son, and called his name Samson. And the child grew, and the Lord blessed him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Spirit of the Lord began to be with him in the camp of Dan, between Saraa and Esthaol.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Samson went down to Thamnatha, and seeing there a woman of the daughters of the Philistines,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He came up, and told his father and his mother, saying: I saw a woman in Thamnatha of the daughters of the Philistines: I beseech you, take her for me to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his father and mother said to him: Is there no woman among the daughters of thy brethren, or among all my people, that thou wilt take a wife of the Philistines, who are uncircumcised? And Samson said to his father: Take this woman for me; for she hath pleased my eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now his parents knew not that the thing was done by the Lord, and that he sought an occasion against the Philistines: for at that time the Philistines had dominion over Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Samson went down with his father and mother to Thamnatha. And when they were come to the vineyards of the town, behold a young lion met him, raging and roaring.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Spirit of the Lord came upon Samson, and he tore the lion as he would have torn a kid in pieces, having nothing at all in his hand: and he would not tell this to his father and mother.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went down, and spoke to the woman that had pleased his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after some days, returning to take her, he went aside to see the carcass of the lion, and behold there was a swarm of bees in the mouth of the lion, and a honey-comb.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had taken it in his hands, he went on eating: and coming to his father and mother, he gave them of it, and they ate: but he would not tell them that he had taken the honey from the body of the lion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So his father went down to the woman, and made a feast for his son Samson: for so the young men used to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the citizens of that place saw him, they brought him thirty companions to be with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Samson said to them: I will propose to you a riddle, which if you declare unto me within the seven days of the feast, I will give you thirty shirts, and as many coats:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if you shall not be able to declare it, you shall give me thirty shirts and the same number of coats. They answered him: Put forth the riddle, that we may hear it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to them: Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness. And they could not for three days expound the riddle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the seventh day came, they said to the wife of Samson: Sooth thy husband, and persuade him to tell thee what the riddle meaneth. But if thou wilt not do it, we will burn thee, and thy father’s house. Have you called us to the wedding on purpose to strip us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So she wept before Samson and complained, saying: Thou hatest me, and dost not love me: therefore thou wilt not expound to me the riddle, which thou hast proposed to the sons of my people. But he answered: I would not tell it to my father and mother: and how can I tell it to thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So she wept before him the seven days of the feast: and, at length, on the seventh day, as she was troublesome to him, he expounded it. And she immediately told her countrymen.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they, on the seventh day before the sun went down, said to him: What is sweeter than honey? and what is stronger than a lion? And he said to them: If you had not ploughed with my heifer, you had not found out my riddle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Spirit of the Lord came upon him, and he went down to Ascalon, and slew there thirty men whose garments he took away, and gave to them that had declared the riddle. And being exceeding angry, he went up to his father’s house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But his wife took one of his friends and bridal companions for her husband.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a while after, when the days of the wheat harvest were at hand, Samson came, meaning to visit his wife, and he brought her a kid of the flock. And when he would have gone into her chamber, as usual, her father would not suffer him, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I thought thou hadst hated her, and therefore I gave her to thy friend: but she hath a sister, who is younger and fairer than she, take her to wife instead of her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Samson answered him: From this day I shall be blameless in what I do against the Philistines: for I will do you evils.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went and caught three hundred foxes, and coupled them tail to tail, and fastened torches between the tails:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And setting them on fire he let the foxes go, that they might run about hither and thither. And they presently went into the standing corn of the Philistines. Which being set on fire, both the corn that was already carried together, and that which was yet standing, was all burnt, insomuch that the flame consumed also the vineyards and the oliveyards.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Philistines said: Who hath done this thing? And it was answered: Samson, the son in law of the Thamnathite, because he took away his wife, and gave her to another, hath done these things. And the Philistines went up and burnt both the woman and her father.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Samson said to them: Although you have done this, yet will I be revenged of you, and then I will be quiet.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he made a great slaughter of them, so that in astonishment they laid the calf of the leg upon the thigh. And going down he dwelt in a cavern of the rock Etam.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Philistines going up into the land of Juda, camped in the place which afterwards was called Lechi, that is, the Jawbone, where their army was spread abroad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the men of the tribe of Juda said to them: Why are you come up against us? They answered: We are come to bind Samson, and to pay him for what he hath done against us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore three thousand men of Juda went down to the cave of the rock Etam, and said to Samson: Knowest thou not that the Philistines rule over us? Why wouldst thou do thus? And he said to them: As they did to me, so have I done to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to him: We are come to bind thee, and to deliver thee into the hands of the Philistines. And Samson said to them: Swear to me, and promise me that you will not kill me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They said: We will not kill thee: but we will deliver thee up bound. And they bound him with two new cords, and brought him from the rock Etam.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when he was come to the place of the Jawbone, and the Philistines shouting went to meet him, the Spirit of the Lord came strongly upon him: and as flax is wont to be consumed at the approach of fire, so the bands with which he was bound were broken and loosed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And finding a jawbone, even the jawbone of an ass, which lay there, catching it up, he slew therewith a thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said: With the jawbone of an ass, with the jaw of the colt of asses, I have destroyed them, and have slain a thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had ended these words, singing, he threw the jawbone out of his hand, and called the name of that place Ramathlechi, which is interpreted the lifting up of the jawbone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being very thirsty, he cried to the Lord, and said: Thou hast given this very great deliverance and victory into the hand of thy servant: and behold I die for thirst, and shall fall into the hands of the uncircumcised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Lord opened a great tooth in the jaw of the ass and waters issued out of it. And when he had drunk them, he refreshed his spirit, and recovered his strength. Therefore the name of that place was called The Spring of him that invoked from the jawbone, until this present day.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he judged Israel, in the days of the Philistines, twenty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He went also into Gaza, and saw there a woman, a harlot, and went in unto her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the Philistines had heard this, and it was noised about among them, that Samson was come into the city, they surrounded him, setting guards at the gate of the city, and watching there all the night in silence, that in the morning they might kill him as he went out.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Samson slept till midnight, and then rising, he took both the doors of the gate, with the posts thereof and the bolt, and laying them on his shoulders, carried them up to the top of the hill, which looketh towards Hebron.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this he loved a woman, who dwelt in the valley of Sorec, and she was called Dalila.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the princes of the Philistines came to her, and said: Deceive him, and learn of him wherein his great strength lieth, and how we may be able to overcome him, to bind and afflict him: which if thou shalt do, we will give thee every one of us eleven hundred pieces of silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dalila said to Samson: Tell me, I beseech thee, wherein thy greatest strength lieth, and what it is, wherewith if thou wert bound, thou couldst not break loose.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Samson answered her: If I shall be bound with seven cords, made of sinews not yet dry, but still moist, I shall be weak like other men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the princes of the Philistines brought unto her seven cords, such as he spoke of, with which she bound him;

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Men lying privately in wait with her, and in the chamber, expecting the event of the thing, and she cried out to him: The Philistines are upon thee, Samson. And he broke the bands, as a man would break a thread of tow twined with spittle, when it smelleth the fire: so it was not known wherein his strength lay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dalila said to him: Behold thou hast mocked me, and hast told me a false thing: but now at least tell me wherewith thou mayest be bound.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered her: If I shall be bound with new ropes, that were never in work, I shall be weak and like other men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dalila bound him again with these, and cried out: The Philistines are upon thee, Samson, there being an ambush prepared for him in the chamber. But he broke the bands like threads of webs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dalila said to him again: How long dost thou deceive me, and tell me lies? Shew me wherewith thou mayest be bound. And Samson answered her: If thou plattest the seven locks of my head with a lace, and tying them round about a nail, fastenest it in the ground, I shall be weak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when Dalila had done this, she said to him: The Philistines are upon thee, Samson. And awaking out of his sleep, he drew out the nail with the hairs and the lace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Dalila said to him: How dost thou say thou lovest me, when thy mind is not with me? Thou hast told me lies these three times, and wouldst not tell me wherein thy greatest strength lieth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when she pressed him much, and continually hung upon him for many days, giving him no time to rest, his soul fainted away, and was wearied even unto death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then opening the truth of the thing, he said to her: The razor hath never come upon my head, for I am a Nazarite, that is to say, consecrated to God from my mother’s womb: If my head be shaven, my strength shall depart from me, and I shall become weak, and shall be like other men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then seeing that he had discovered to her all his mind, she sent to the princes of the Philistines, saying: Come up this once more, for now he hath opened his heart to me. And they went up, taking with them the money which they had promised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But she made him sleep upon her knees, and lay his head in her bosom. And she called a barber and shaved his seven locks, and began to drive him away, and thrust him from her: for immediately his strength departed from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she said: The Philistines are upon thee, Samson. And awaking from sleep, he said in his mind: I will go out as I did before, and shake myself, not knowing that the Lord was departed from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Philistines seized upon him, and forthwith pulled out his eyes, and led him bound in chains to Gaza, and shutting him up in prison made him grind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now his hair began to grow again,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the princes of the Philistines assembled together, to offer great sacrifices to Dagon their god, and to make merry, saying: Our god hath delivered our enemy Samson into our hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the people also seeing this, praised their god, and said the same: Our god hath delivered our adversary into our hands, him that destroyed our country, and killed very many.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And rejoicing in their feasts, when they had now taken their good cheer, they commanded that Samson should be called, and should play before them. And being brought out of prison, he played before them; and they made him stand between two pillars.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to the lad that guided his steps: Suffer me to touch the pillars which support the whole house, and let me lean upon them, and rest a little.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the house was full of men and women, and all the princes of the Philistines were there. Moreover about three thousand persons of both sexes, from the roof and the higher part of the house, were beholding Samson’s play.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he called upon the Lord, saying: O Lord God remember me, and restore to me now my former strength, O my God, that I may revenge myself on my enemies, and for the loss of my two eyes I may take one revenge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And laying hold on both the pillars on which the house rested, and holding the one with his right hand, and the other with his left,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He said: Let me die with the Philistines. And when he had strongly shook the pillars, the house fell upon all the princes, and the rest of the multitude, that was there: and he killed many more at his death, than he had killed before in his life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his brethren and all his kindred, going down took his body, and buried it between Saraa and Esthaol, in the buryingplace of his father Manue: and he judged Israel twenty years.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was at that time a man of mount Ephraim, whose name was Michas.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who said to his mother: The eleven hundred pieces of silver, which thou hadst put aside for thyself, and concerning which thou didst swear in my hearing, behold I have, and they are with me. And she said to him. Blessed be my son by the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So he restored them to his mother, who said to him: I have consecrated and vowed this silver to the Lord, that my son may receive it at my hand, and make a graven and a molten god; so now I deliver it to thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he restored them to his mother: and she took two hundred pieces of silver and gave them to the silversmith, to make of them a graven and a molten god, which was in the house of Michas.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he separated also therein a little temple for the god, and made an ephod, and theraphim, that is to say, a priestly garment, and idols: and he filled the hand of one of his sons, and he became his priest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days there was no king in Israel, but every one did that which seemed right to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was also another young man of Bethlehem Juda, of the kindred thereof: and he was a Levite, and dwelt there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now he went out from the city of Bethlehem, and desired to sojourn wheresoever he should find it convenient for him. And when he was come to mount Ephraim, as he was on his journey, and had turned aside a little into the house of Michas,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He was asked by him whence he came. And he answered: I am a Levite of Bethlehem Juda, and I am going to dwell where I can, and where I shall find a place to my advantage.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Michas said: Stay with me, and be unto me a father and a priest, and I will give thee every year ten pieces of silver, and a double suit of apparel, and thy victuals.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He was content, and abode with the man, and was unto him as one of his sons.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Michas filled his hand, and had the young man with him for his priest, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now I know God will do me good, since I have a priest of the race of the Levites.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In those days there was no king in Israel, and the tribe of Dan sought them an inheritance to dwell in: for unto that day they had not received their lot among the other tribes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the children of Dan sent five most valiant men, of their stock and family, from Saraa and Esthaol, to spy out the land, and to view it diligently: and they said to them: Go, and view the land. They went on their way, and when they came to mount Ephraim, they went into the house of Michas, and rested there:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And knowing the voice of the young man the Levite, and lodging with him, they said to him: Who brought thee hither? what dost thou here? why wouldst thou come hither?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered them: Michas hath done such and such things for me, and hath hired me to be his priest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they desired him to consult the Lord, that they might know whether their journey should be prosperous, and the thing should have effect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered them: Go in peace: the Lord looketh on your way, and the journey that you go.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So the five men going on came to Lais: and they saw how the people dwelt therein without any fear, according to the custom of the Sidonians, secure and easy, having no man at all to oppose them, being very rich, and living separated, at a distance from Sidon and from all men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they returned to their brethren in Saraa and Esthaol, who asked them what they had done: to whom they answered:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arise, and let us go up to them: for we have seen the land which is exceeding rich and fruitful: neglect not, lose no time: let us go and possess it, there will be no difficulty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We shall come to a people that is secure, into a spacious country, and the Lord will deliver the place to us, in which there is no want of any thing that groweth on the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There went therefore of the kindred of Dan, to wit, from Saraa and Esthaol, six hundred men, furnished with arms for war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going up they lodged in Cariathiarim of Juda: which place from that time is called the camp of Dan, and is behind Cariathiarim.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: From thence they passed into mount Ephraim. And when they were come to the house of Michas,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The five men, that before had been sent to view the land of Lais, said to the rest of their brethren: You know that in these houses there is an ephod and theraphim, and a graven and a molten god: see what you are pleased to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they had turned a little aside, they went into the house of the young man the Levite, who was in the house of Michas: and they saluted him with words of peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the six hundred men stood before the door, appointed with their arms.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But they that were gone into the house of the young man, went about to take away the graven god, and the ephod, and the theraphim, and the molten god, and the priest stood before the door, the six hundred valiant men waiting not far off.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they that were gone in took away the graven thing, the ephod, and the idols, and the molten god, And the priest said to them: What are you doing?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they said to him: Hold thy peace, and put thy finger on thy mouth, and come with us, that we may have thee for a father, and a priest. Whether is better for thee, to be a priest in the house of one man, or in a tribe and family in Israel?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When he heard this, he agreed to their words, and took the ephod, and the idols, and the graven god, and departed with them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they were going forward, and had put before them the children and the cattle, and all that was valuable,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And were now at a distance from the house of Michas, the men that dwelt in the houses of Michas gathering together followed them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And began to shout out after them. They looked back, and said to Michas: What aileth thee? Why dost thou cry?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he answered: You have taken away my gods which I have made me, and the priest, and all that I have, and do you say: What aileth thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Dan said to him: See thou say no more to us, lest men enraged come upon thee, and thou perish with all thy house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so they went on the journey they had begun. But Michas seeing that they were stronger than he, returned to his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the six hundred men took the priest, and the things we spoke of before, and came to Lais, to a people that was quiet and secure, and smote them with the edge of the sword: and the city they burnt with fire,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There being no man at all who brought them any succour, because they dwelt far from Sidon, and had no society or business with any man. And the city was in the land of Rohob: and they rebuilt it, and dwelt therein,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Calling the name of the city Dan, after the name of their father, who was the son of Israel, which before was called Lais.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they set up to themselves the graven idol, and Jonathan the son of Gersam, the son of Moses, he and his sons were priests in the tribe of Dan, until the day of their captivity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the idol of Michas remained with them all the time that the house of God was in Silo. In those days there was no king in Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There was a certain Levite, who dwelt on the side of mount Ephraim, who took a wife of Bethlehem Juda:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And she left him, and returned to her father’s house in Bethlehem, and abode with him four months.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And her husband followed her, willing to be reconciled with her, and to speak kindly to her, and to bring her back with him, having with him a servant and two asses: and she received him, and brought him into her father’s house. And when his father in law had heard this, and had seen him, he met him with joy,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And embraced the man. And the son in law tarried in the house of his father in law three days, eating with him and drinking familiarly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But on the fourth day, arising early in the morning, he desired to depart. But his father in law kept him, and said to him: Taste first a little bread, and strengthen thy stomach, and so thou shalt depart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sat down together, and ate and drank. And the father of the young woman said to his son in law: I beseech thee to stay here to day, and let us make merry together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But he rising up, began to be for departing. And nevertheless his father in law earnestly pressed him, and made him stay with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when morning was come, the Levite prepared to go on his journey. And his father in law said to him again: I beseech thee to take a little meat, and strengthening thyself, till the day be farther advanced, afterwards thou mayest depart. And they ate together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the young man arose to set forward with his wife and servant. And his father in law spoke to him again: Consider that the day is declining, and draweth toward evening: tarry with me to day also, and spend the day in mirth, and to morrow thou shalt depart, that thou mayest go into thy house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His son in law would not consent to his words: but forthwith went forward, and came over against Jebus, which by another name is called Jerusalem, leading with him two asses laden, and his concubine.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now they were come near Jebus, and the day was far spent: and the servant said to his master: Come, I beseech thee, let us turn into the city of the Jebusites, and lodge there.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His master answered him: I will not go into the town of another nation, who are not of the children of Israel, but I will pass over to Gabaa:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when I shall come thither, we will lodge there, or at least in the city of Rama.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they passed by Jebus, and went on their journey, and the sun went down upon them when they were by Gabaa, which is in the tribe of Benjamin:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they turned into it to lodge there. And when they were come in, they sat in the street of the city, for no man would receive them to lodge.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold they saw an old man, returning out of the field and from his work in the evening, and he also was of mount Ephraim, and dwelt as a stranger in Gabaa; but the men of that country were the children of Jemini.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the old man lifting up his eyes, saw the man sitting with his bundles in the street of the city, and said to him: Whence comest thou? and whither goest thou?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered him: We came out from Bethlehem Juda, and we are going to our home, which is on the side of mount Ephraim, from whence we went to Bethlehem: and now we go to the house of God, and none will receive us under his roof:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We have straw and hay for provender of the asses, and bread and wine for the use of myself and of thy handmaid, and of the servant that is with me: we want nothing but lodging.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the old man answered him: Peace be with thee: I will furnish all things that are necessary: only I beseech thee, stay not in the street.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he brought him into his house, and gave provender to his asses: and after they had washed their feet, he entertained them with a feast.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: While they were making merry, and refreshing their bodies with meat and drink, after the labour of the journey, the men of that city, sons of Belial (that is, without yoke), came and beset the old man’s house, and began to knock at the door, calling to the master of the house, and saying: Bring forth the man that came into thy house, that we may abuse him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the old man went out to them, and said: Do not so, my brethren, do not so wickedly: because this man is come into my lodging, and cease I pray you from this folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have a maiden daughter, and this man hath a concubine, I will bring them out to you, and you may humble them, and satisfy your lust: only, I beseech you, commit not this crime against nature on the man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They would not be satisfied with his words; which the man seeing, brought out his concubine to them, and abandoned her to their wickedness: and when they had abused her all the night, they let her go in the morning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the woman, at the dawning of the day, came to the door of the house, where her lord lodged, and there fell down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in the morning the man arose, and opened the door, that he might end the journey he had begun: and behold his concubine lay before the door with her hands spread on the threshold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He thinking she was taking her rest, said to her: Arise, and let us be going. But as she made no answer, perceiving she was dead, he took her up, and laid her upon his ass, and returned to his house.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come home, he took a sword, and divided the dead body of his wife with her bones into twelve parts, and sent the pieces into all the borders of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when every one had seen this, they all cried out: There was never such a thing done in Israel, from the day that our fathers came up out of Egypt, until this day: give sentence, and decree in common what ought to be done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then all the children of Israel went out, and gathered together as one man, from Dan to Bersabee, with the land of Galaad, to the Lord in Maspha:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the chiefs of the people, and all the tribes of Israel, met together in the assembly of the people of God, four hundred thousand footmen fit for war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: (Nor were the children of Benjamin ignorant that the children of Israel were come up to Maspha.) And the Levite, the husband of the woman that was killed being asked, how so great a wickedness had been committed,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Answered: I came into Gabaa, of Benjamin, with my wife, and there I lodged:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold the men of that city, in the night beset the house wherein I was, intending to kill me, and abused my wife with an incredible fury of lust, so that at last she died.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I took her and cut her in pieces, and sent the parts into all the borders of your possession: because there never was so heinous a crime, and so great an abomination committed in Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: You are all here, O children of Israel, determine what you ought to do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the people standing, answered as by the voice of one man: We will not return to our tents, neither shall any one of us go into his own house:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But this we will do in common against Gabaa:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We will take ten men of a hundred out of all the tribes of Israel, and a hundred out of a thousand, and a thousand out of ten thousand, to bring victuals for the army, that we may fight against Gabaa of Benjamin, and render to it for its wickedness, what it deserveth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all Israel were gathered together against the city, as one man, with one mind, and one counsel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sent messengers to all the tribe of Benjamin, to say to them: Why hath so great an abomination been found among you?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver up the men of Gabaa, that have committed this heinous crime, that they may die, and the evil may be taken away out of Israel. But they would not hearken to the proposition of their brethren the children of Israel:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But out of all the cities which were of their lot, they gathered themselves together into Gabaa, to aid them, and to fight against the whole people of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were found of Benjamin five and twenty thousand men that drew the sword, besides the inhabitants of Gabaa,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who were seven hundred most valiant men, fighting with the left hand as well as with the right: and slinging stones so sure that they could hit even a hair, and not miss by the stone’s going on either side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Of the men of Israel also, beside the children of Benjamin, were found four hundred thousand that drew swords and were prepared to fight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they arose and came to the house of God, that is, to Silo: and they consulted God, and said: Who shall be in our army the first to go to the battle against the children of Benjamin? And the Lord answered them: Let Juda be your leader.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And forthwith the children of Israel rising in the morning, camped by Gabaa:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And going out from thence to fight against Benjamin, began to assault the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Benjamin coming out of Gabaa slew of the children of Israel that day two and twenty thousand men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again Israel, trusting in their strength and their number, set their army in array in the same place, where they had fought before:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yet so that they first went up and wept before the Lord until night: and consulted him and said: Shall I go out any more to fight against the children of Benjamin my brethren or not? And he answered them: Go up against them, and join battle.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the children of Israel went out the next day to fight against the children of Benjamin,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The children of Benjamin sallied forth out of the gates of Gabaa: and meeting them, made so great a slaughter of them, as to kill eighteen thousand men that drew the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore all the children of Israel came to the house of God, and sat and wept before the Lord: and they fasted that day till the evening, and offered to him holocausts, and victims of peace offerings,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And inquired of him concerning their state. At that time the ark of the covenant of the Lord was there,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Phinees, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, was over the house. So they consulted the Lord, and said: Shall we go out any more to fight against the children of Benjamin, our brethren, or shall we cease? And the Lord said to them: Go up, for to morrow I will deliver them into your hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel set ambushes round about the city of Gabaa:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they drew up their army against Benjamin the third time, as they had done the first and second.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Benjamin boldly issued out of the city, and seeing their enemies flee, pursued them a long way, so as to wound and kill some of them, as they had done the first and second day, whilst they fled by two highways, whereof one goeth up to Bethel and the other to Gabaa, and they slew about thirty men:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they thought to cut them off as they did before. But they artfully feigning a flight, designed to draw them away from the city, and by their seeming to flee, to bring them to the highways aforesaid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then all the children of Israel rising up out of the places where they were, set their army in battle array, in the place which is called Baalthamar. The ambushes also, which were about the city, began by little and little to come forth,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to march from the west side of the city. And other ten thousand men chosen out of all Israel, attacked the inhabitants of the city. And the battle grew hot against the children of Benjamin: and they understood not that present death threatened them on every side.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord defeated them before the children of Israel, and they slew of them in that day five and twenty thousand, and one hundred, all fighting men, and that drew the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the children of Benjamin, when they saw themselves to be too weak, began to flee. Which the children of Israel seeing, gave them place to flee, that they might come to the ambushes that were prepared, which they had set near the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were in ambush arose on a sudden out of their coverts, and whilst Benjamin turned their backs to the slayers, went into the city, and smote it with the edge of the sword.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the children of Israel had given a sign to them, whom they had laid in ambushes, that after they had taken the city, they should make a fire: that by the smoke rising on high, they might shew that the city was taken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the children of Israel saw this in the battle, (for the children of Benjamin thought they fled, and pursued them vigorously, killing thirty men of their army)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And perceived, as it were, a pillar of smoke rise up from the city; and Benjamin looking back, saw that the city was taken, and that the flames ascended on high:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They that before had made as if they fled, turning their faces, stood bravely against them. Which the children of Benjamin seeing, turned their backs,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And began to go towards the way of the desert, the enemy pursuing them thither also. And they that fired the city came also out to meet them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so it was, that they were slain on both sides by the enemies, and there was no rest of their men dying. They fell and were beaten down on the east side of the city of Gabaa.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they that were slain in the same place, were eighteen thousand men, all most valiant soldiers.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when they that remained of Benjamin saw this, they fled into the wilderness, and made towards the rock that is called Remmon. In that flight also, as they were straggling, and going different ways; they slew of them five thousand men. And as they went farther, they still pursued them, and slew also other two thousand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And so it came to pass, that all that were slain of Benjamin, in divers places, were five and twenty thousand fighting men, most valiant for war.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there remained of all the number of Benjamin only six hundred men that were able to escape, and flee to the wilderness: and they abode in the rock Remmon four months.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the children of Israel returning, put all the remains of the city to the sword, both men and beasts, and all the cities and villages of Benjamin were consumed with devouring flames.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the children of Israel had also sworn in Maspha, saying: None of us shall give of his daughters to the children of Benjamin to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they all came to the house of God in Silo, and sitting before him till the evening, lifted up their voices, and began to lament and weep, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Lord God of Israel, why is so great an evil come to pass in thy people, that this day one tribe should be taken away from among us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And rising early the next day, they built an altar: and offered there holocausts, and victims of peace, and they said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is there among all the tribes of Israel that came not up with the army of the Lord? for they had bound themselves with a great oath, when they were in Maspha, that whosoever were wanting should be slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Israel being moved with repentance for their brother Benjamin, began to say: One tribe is taken away from Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whence shall they take wives? For we have all in general sworn, not to give our daughters to them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore they said: Who is there of all the tribes of Israel, that came not up to the Lord to Maspha? And, behold, the inhabitants of Jabes Galaad were found not to have been in that army.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: (At that time also when they were in Silo, no one of them was found there,)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they sent ten thousand of the most valiant men, and commanded them, saying: Go and put the inhabitants of Jabes Galaad to the sword, with their wives and their children.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this is what you shall observe: Every male, and all women that have known men, you shall kill, but the virgins you shall save.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And there were found of Jabes Galaad four hundred virgins, that had not known the bed of a man, and they brought them to the camp in Silo, into the land of Chanaan.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they sent messengers to the children of Benjamin, that were in the rock Remmon, and commanded them to receive them in peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Benjamin came at that time, and wives were given them of the daughters of Jabes Galaad: but they found no others, whom they might give in like manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all Israel was very sorry, and repented for the destroying of one tribe out of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the ancients said: What shall we do with the rest, that have not received wives? for all the women in Benjamin are dead.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we must use all care, and provide with great diligence, that one tribe be not destroyed out of Israel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as to our own daughters we cannot give them, being bound with an oath and a curse, whereby we said: Cursed be he that shall give Benjamin any of his daughters to wife.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So they took counsel, and said: Behold, there is a yearly solemnity of the Lord in Silo, which is situate on the north of the city of Bethel, and on the east side of the way, that goeth from Bethel to Sichem, and on the south of the town of Lebona.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they commanded the children of Benjamin and said: Go, and lie hid in the vineyards,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when you shall see the daughters of Silo come out, as the custom is, to dance, come ye on a sudden out of the vineyards, and catch you every man his wife among them, and go into the land of Benjamin.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when their fathers and their brethren shall come, and shall begin to complain against you, and to chide, we will say to them: Have pity on them: for they took them not away as by the right of war or conquest, but when they asked to have them, you gave them not, and the fault was committed on your part.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the children of Benjamin did as they had been commanded: and, according to their number, they carried off for themselves every man his wife of them that were dancing: and they went into their possession, and built up their cities, and dwelt in them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The children of Israel also returned by their tribes, and families, to their dwellings. In those days there was no king in Israel: but every one did that which seemed right to himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
